title: Work-order dispatch architecture — acting agents run as pi subprocesses; executeAgent stays the classify/structured-output primitive
context: FEAT-014's first acceptance criterion requires the dispatch-architecture choice to be filed as a decision before implementation. The underdetermined options (analysis/2026-07-07-work-order-dispatch-dogfood-breaks.md §cross-cutting, restated in FEAT-014 and FGAP-124): (a) dispatch target agents as pi subprocesses, where real tools exist; (b) grow executeAgent a bound tool-execution loop; (c) re-scope work-orders to output-only agents, contradicting the schema's scope contract. Implementation shipped option (a) on main (merge 8e2e764e, verified end to end per FGAP-124 closure) without this decision being filed — surfaced by the 2026-07-07 currency audit; filed retroactively under user grant to record the enacted choice.
decision: Acting work-order agents are dispatched as pi subprocesses (pi --mode json -p <task> --tools <composed grant> --model <spec>), the pi-workflows production mechanism; the composed capability clamp becomes the subprocess --tools allowlist so it reaches execution. executeAgent remains a single-turn classify/structured-output primitive and is not grown a tool-execution loop. Work-orders are not re-scoped to output-only.
consequences: Real tools exist on the work-order dispatch path (WO-001 probe recipe passes end to end non-interactively; FGAP-124 closed).

The scope clamp leg of FGAP-125 is satisfied via the --tools allowlist; the remaining FGAP-125 legs (context_blocks injection, output_contract validation) must cross the process boundary under this shape.
FGAP-127 (agents-tier seeding) and FGAP-126 (non-interactive gating) proceed against subprocess dispatch.
Every future in-process executeAgent consumer inherits the primitive's no-tools ceiling by design; agents whose job is to act must route through subprocess dispatch.
